2 min read
Just now

--

HTML (HyperText Markup Language) is the fundamental language used to create web pages on the internet. Its history began in 1989 at CERN, a scientific research organization in Switzerland. At that time, researchers faced difficulties sharing information because different computers used incompatible systems.
To solve this problem, Tim Berners-Lee developed the concept of the World Wide Web and created HTML in 1991. The early version of HTML was very simple, containing only basic tags such as headings, paragraphs, and hyperlinks. Its main purpose was to structure information and connect documents rather than focus on design or appearance.
As the internet grew, HTML evolved significantly. In 1995, HTML 2.0 became the first official standard. Later versions such as HTML 3.2 and HTML 4.01 introduced more advanced features, including tables, forms, and better support for scripts. During this period, the development and standardization of HTML were managed by the World Wide Web Consortium (W3C) World Wide Web Consortium.
In the early 2000s, XHTML was introduced to make HTML more strict and structured. However, it was considered too rigid for modern web development. As a result, the WHATWG WHATWG continued developing HTML in a more flexible and practical way.
This led to the creation of HTML5, which became a major breakthrough. HTML5 introduced powerful features such as video and audio support, canvas for graphics, and improved tools for building mobile-friendly and interactive web applications.
Today, HTML is no longer developed in fixed versions. Instead, it follows a “living standard” approach, meaning it is continuously updated to meet the needs of modern web technology.
